coredata.dc:title: Halogen-controlled engineering of fluorescent carbon dots with adjustable color for food freshness detection
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Minghui
Zheng, Xiaochun
Li, Cheng
Zheng, Zixin
Song, Guangchun
Xu, Le
Chen, Li
Zhang, Dequan
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon quantum dot-based visualization methods for food freshness analysis have gained increasing attention. However, traditional carbon quantum dots (CDs) exhibit single-color fluorescence and are prone to background light interference. Herein, a novel series of multi-color fluorescent CDs is successfully synthesized through a one-pot method, using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) ligands of the same halogen family as the precursor and citric acid as the carbon source. The CDs doped with different halogens exhibit a distinct color change from blue to red, attributed to differences in the key fluorophore molecules of corresponding pyridone derivatives. The halogen-doped CDs display pH-responsive characteristics based on color change sensitivity, enabling their use as putrescine-sensing probes. Excitingly, the chlorine-doped ratiometric fluorescent CDs (ClOPD-CDs) show the most significant color change (ΔE = 44.66), delivering a stark color shift from orange-red to yellow based on the deprotonation of edge amino groups in the presence of biogenic amine molecules. Furthermore, the relationship between total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) and RGB outputs of a homemade fluorescent biosensor for meat spoilage (R² = 0.9386) confirms that ClOPD-CDs function effectively as an on-line fluorochromic probe for visual and quantitative food freshness monitoring via smartphone. The ratiometric fluorescence signals have facilitated the development of a smartphone-compatible sensing application, anticipated to provide a convenient tool for real-time food quality detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Freshness
Food

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectral detection of putrescine in aqueous solution using CLOPD-CDs ratiometric probe
Calculation of the HOMO energy level

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key R&D Program of Shandong Province
National Key R&D Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Key R&D Program of Shandong Province, China (2023CXGC010708), and National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFD2100500).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and morphology characterizations. (a) Synthesis scheme for CDs. (b–f) TEM and HRTEM images of OPD-CDs, FOPD-CDs, ClOPD-CDs, BrOPD-CDs, and IOPD-CDs. (g-k) Particle size distributions of OPD-CDs, FOPD-CDs, ClOPD-CDs, BrOPD-CDs, and IOPD-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of the (a) OPD-CDs and (b) other selected CDs.
(a-e) Full XPS spectrum of OPD-CDs, FOPD-CDs, ClOPD-CDs, BrOPD-CDs, and IOPD-CDs. XPS high-resolution (f-j) C 1 s and (k-o) N 1 s spectra of OPD-CDs, FOPD-CDs, ClOPD-CDs, BrOPD-CDs, and IOPD-CDs.

UV–vis absorption spectra and characteristic excitation (ex) and emission (em) peaks of (a) OPD-CDs, (b) FOPD-CDs, (c) ClOPD-CDs, (d) BrOPD-CDs, and (e) IOPD-CDs (the insets are digital photos under UV irradiation at 302 nm). (f) Schematic representation of possible formation mechanism for CDs with tunable PL emission.

(a) HOMO–LUMO energy levels of ClDPCA. (b-c) HOMO–LUMO energy levels of fluorescence molecules in ClOPD-CDs and OPD-CDs, respectively. (d) A schematic of multi-color CDs building-up process in the solvent-free method system.

(a) Photographs of the five selected CDs exposed to 50 mg/L putrescine under 302 nm UV lamps. (b) Fluorescence response (λex = 470 nm) and corresponding photographs under a 302 nm UV lamp of ClOPD-CDs to different concentration of putrescine (from left to right, the concentrations of putrescine in cuvette are 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, and 50 mg/L, respectively). The insets are the Calibration curve of fluorescence intensity ratio (I554/I608) of ClOPD-CDs versus concentration of putrescine. (c) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I554/I608) of ClOPD-CDs versus concentration of other BAs.

Exploration of detection mechanism. (a) Zeta potential of the five selected CDs exposed to 50 mg/L putrescine. (b) TRPL spectra of the ClOPD-CDs. (c) Normalized 3D FL spectra of ClOPD-CDs under different pH condition. (d) Mechanism of the red PL emission of the ClOPD-CDs.

Changes in ClOPD-CDs energy levels due to protonation. (a−c) Full-scale UPS, near Fermi energy (low binding energy), and secondary electron cut-off (high binding energy) regions for ClOPD-CDs prior to protonation. (d−f) Full-scale UPS, near Fermi energy, and secondary electron cut-off regions post-protonation. (g) UV−Vis spectra comparing ClOPD-CDs before and after protonation. (h-i) Internal energy level shifts from protonation (h: ClOPD-CDs without protonation; i: ClOPD-CDs with edge protonation).

(a) Diagram illustrating meat freshness detection using a fluorescent biosensor and smartphone. (b) Fluorescent biosensor tracking pork, beef, and mutton freshness at 25℃ with visible fluorescent color changes. Fluorescence images captured under 302 nm UV light. TVB-N values for (c) pork, (d) beef, and (e) mutton at 25℃ over various time periods.

head.ce:title: Halogen-controlled engineering of fluorescent carbon dots with adjustable color for food freshness detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Food Science and Technology, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Key Laboratory of Agro-products Quality and Safety Control in Storage and Transport Process, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Beijing 100193, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Food Science and Technology, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Key Laboratory of Agro-products Quality and Safety Control in Storage and Transport Process, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Food Science and Technology, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Key Laboratory of Agro-products Quality and Safety Control in Storage and Transport Process, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Beijing 100193, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Institute of Food Science and Technology, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Beijing 100193, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Food Science and Technology, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dot-based visualization methods for food freshness analysis have gained increasing attention. However, traditional carbon quantum dots (CDs) exhibit single-color fluorescence and are prone to background light interference. Herein, a novel series of multi-color fluorescent CDs is successfully synthesized through a one-pot method, using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) ligands of the same halogen family as the precursor and citric acid as the carbon source. The CDs doped with different halogens exhibit a distinct color change from blue to red, attributed to differences in the key fluorophore molecules of corresponding pyridone derivatives. The halogen-doped CDs display pH-responsive characteristics based on color change sensitivity, enabling their use as putrescine-sensing probes. Excitingly, the chlorine-doped ratiometric fluorescent CDs (ClOPD-CDs) show the most significant color change (ΔE = 44.66), delivering a stark color shift from orange-red to yellow based on the deprotonation of edge amino groups in the presence of biogenic amine molecules. Furthermore, the relationship between total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) and RGB outputs of a homemade fluorescent biosensor for meat spoilage (R² = 0.9386) confirms that ClOPD-CDs function effectively as an on-line fluorochromic probe for visual and quantitative food freshness monitoring via smartphone. The ratiometric fluorescence signals have facilitated the development of a smartphone-compatible sensing application, anticipated to provide a convenient tool for real-time food quality detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food quality and safety are intimately linked to public health outcomes. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the consumption of unsafe food can result in over 200 diseases, ranging from diarrhea to cancer. Food spoilage is particularly concerning as it causes millions of illnesses and hundreds of thousands of deaths each year [1]. Putrescine, a biogenic amine (BA), is an important indicator of spoilage. It is produced through bacterial metabolism in protein-rich foods—such as seafood, fish, meat, and fermented products—via amino acid decarboxylation [2]. Given the risks posed by BAs like putrescine, it is critical and urgent to investigate simple, yet effective techniques for their detection, ensuring the monitoring of food quality.

Fluorescent sensing techniques prove to be an noteworthy tool in food quality detection due to their non-destructive, rapid, and highly sensitive features. Various fluorescent materials have been developed, including fluorescent metal-organic frameworks [3], semiconductor quantum dots [4], organic dyes [5], and fluorescent metal nanoclusters [6]. Among these probe materials, carbon quantum dots (CDs) hold substantial promise owing to their straightforward synthesis and superior selectivity. CDs, derived from carbon sources, are typically spherical or near-spherical nanomaterials, measuring 1–10 nm in size, and consist mainly of a carbon core with surface groups. However, their fluorescent emissions generally fall within the blue-green light region, making them susceptible to interference from background light [7]. Thus, achieving long-wavelength and multicolor emissions from CDs is crucial for advancing applications, particularly in biologically relevant detection fields.

Regulating the wide color gamut of fluorescent CDs remains challenging. Since Jiang et al. reported the development of blue, green, and red emissive CDs derived from phenylenediamines, significant breakthroughs have been achieved in the preparation of red emissive CDs [8]. These small aromatic molecules can create large sp² domains with a narrow energy gap, facilitating the tuning of emission and excitation towards longer wavelengths [9]. Two effective regulation methods include surface modification and heteroatom doping. While surface modification can improve optical properties through functional ligands, it typically requires multiple steps, making the process tedious [10]. In contrast, heteroatom doping enables the one-step synthesis of CDs from precursors containing different heteroatoms. Numerous studies have shown that doping with nitrogen (N) [11], fluorine (F) [12,13], sulfur (S) [14], phosphorus (P) [15], and their combinations significantly influence the photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs. For example, N-doped CDs synthesized using benzene-ammonia derivatives as the nitrogen source demonstrate higher luminous efficiency and tunable luminescence [16,17]. Halogen doping can introduce additional energy levels, producing multicolor fluorescence and enhancing both the structural and luminescence properties of CDs [10]. Understanding the PL mechanism provides specific insights for the synthesis and applications of CDs. However, most studies laboriously adjust reaction times and reactants to achieve CDs with long-wavelength emission, a time-consuming and complex process that lacks clear regularity [8,18]. Consequently, formulating an effective and simple strategy for synthesizing multicolor CDs for food quality determination poses a significant challenge and is crucial for an intrinsic understanding of their emission mechanism.

In this study, a novel one-pot synthesis of fluorescent CDs functionalized with o-phenylenediamine (OPD)-type ligands of the same halogen family, achieving polychromatic fluorescent properties that span the entire visible spectrum under UV excitation. Various characterizations showed that the halogens on the aromatic rings of the ligand play a significant role in the red-shift of CDs. With chlorine being incorporated, the obtained ClOPD-CDs probes exhibited superb sensitivity to targeted BAs, along with a significantly perceivable fluorescence color change compared to existing materials. Remarkably, the deposition of ClOPD-CDs on filter paper to create a portable fluorescent sensor enabled successful visual monitoring of meat freshness using the RGB digital mode. This work may inform improvements in food supply chain management and potentially reduce food wastage, consequently bearing substantial economic value.

Citric acid (CA), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), 4-fluoro-o-phenylenediamine (FOPD), 4-chloro-o-phenylenediamine (ClOPD), 4-bromo-1,2-benzenediamine (BrOPD), and 4-iodo-o-phenylenediamine (IOPD) were procured from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethanol was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Whatman No. 1 filter paper was sourced from Shanghai Jinpan Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Pork, beef, and mutton were purchased from local supermarkets in Beijing, China.

216 mg of OPD and 50 mg of CA were dissolved in 30 mL of 50 % ethanol aqueous solution (v:v) using ultrasonic treatment. The solution was subsequently transferred to a 50 mL Teflon reactor and heated at 220℃ for 6 h in an air oven. Upon completion, the reaction mixture was allowed to cool naturally and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove precipitates, yielding a supernatant containing OPD-CDs. This supernatant was further filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate large particles, and the OPD-CDs were purified using ultrafiltration spin columns (1 kD) to obtain a pure OPD-CDs solution. The final solution was stored in the dark at 4℃ for later use. The remaining four CDs were synthesized similarly, with 216 mg of OPD substituted by equivalent amounts of FOPD, ClOPD, BrOPD, and IOPD, labeled as FOPD-CDs, ClOPD-CDs, BrOPD-CDs, and IOPD-CDs, respectively. The concentration of the CDs solution was determined by: (1) placing 1 mL of the solution into an empty tube with a known mass; (2) freeze-drying the solution, then weighing the tube containing the CDs; (3) calculating the mass of the dry CDs; and (4) determining the concentration based on the solution volume and the dry mass of the CDs.

The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using an Avatar FT-IR 360 instrument from Nicolet, USA. Fluorescence spectra were obtained with a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer, provided by Shimadzu, Japan. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured with a JEOL-2010 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Zeta potential measurements were conducted using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Panalytical, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed with an ESCALAB Xi+ spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA, and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) utilized an ESCALAB 250Xi instrument from the same manufacturer. The photoluminescence lifetimes were measured using an FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK).

All calculations were performed using Gaussian 16 A.03 software. The B3LYP functional was used with Grimme’s D3(BJ) dispersion correction. For geometry optimization and frequency calculations, the def2-SVP basis set was applied to all atoms. The structure of the first excited singlet state (S1) was optimized using time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) at the same level, and the transition properties between the ground state (S0) and excited state (S1) were derived. Wave function analyses were executed with Multiwfn 3.8(dev) and isosurface maps were rendered using the VMD visualization program, based on files exported from Multiwfn.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral detection of putrescine in aqueous solution using CLOPD-CDs ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: Forty microliters of ClOPD-CDs aqueous solution was added to a 2 mL calibrated test tube, followed by sequential additions of different amounts of putrescine standard solutions. The mixture was diluted to 2 mL with ultrapure water and mixed thoroughly. After five minutes, the mixture was transferred to a 3 mL quartz cuvette, and fluorescence spectra were recorded. Visual quantification of putrescine solution by smartphone-based analytical device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the HOMO energy level
ce:section.ce:para: This calculation was performed as described elsewhere [7,19]. First, a linear fit of the UPS spectrum region near the Fermi energy was achieved, and the intersection point between the linear fit of the UPS spectrum and the baseline was identified as the E Fermi. Next, the E cutoff was determined similarly in the electron cut-off regions. Finally, the HOMO energy level of the CDs was calculated using the equation: HOMO (eV) = E Incident photon – E cutoff + E Fermi.

Whatman No. 1 filter paper was cut into 1 cm diameter circular pieces. These paper pieces were then soaked in a ClOPD-CDs solution with a concentration of 5.0 mg/mL for 30 min. After soaking, the bioactive paper was processed at 37℃ for approximately 15 min until fully dried. The resulting dried paper base, impregnated with the ClOPD-CDs, served as the fluorescent biosensor for subsequent experiments.

Pork, beef, and mutton samples were collected for monitoring. Both samples and the biosensor were placed in a polypropylene tray without contact (Fig. S1). The samples were monitored at 25℃, during which the fluorescence color change of the biosensor was recorded with a smartphone. Quantitative discrimination of the signal biosensor system was achieved by measuring fluorescence intensity. For comparison, the freshness of the three samples stored at 25℃ was verified by the TVB-N method according to the national standard of the P.R. China (GB 5009.228–2016, GB 2707–2016).

The synthesis of CDs involved solvothermal treatment of a reaction precursor at 220℃ for 6 h, followed by simple purification ( Fig. 1a). A crucial step in synthesizing multicolor CDs involved the functionalization with surface OPD-type ligands, resulting in visually distinct luminescence ranging from blue to red. This indicates that the halogen substituents on the aromatic rings of the ligands play a key role in producing long-wavelength emissions [10]. Notably, we conducted a control experiment using OPD and CA as precursors to synthesize CDs for halogen removal. The product, named OPD-CDs, exhibited similar yellow PL properties as the FOPD-CDs. The microstructure and morphology of the multicolor CDs (OPD-CDs, FOPD-CDs, ClOPD-CDs, BrOPD-CDs, and IOPD-CDs) were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1b-f, the multicolor CDs displayed a uniform distribution, indicating well-dispersed CDs in aqueous solution without noticeable agglomeration. Additionally, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (inset, Fig. 1b-f) revealed distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.22 nm, consistent with the graphitic carbon (100) diffraction plane, suggesting a structure similar to graphite. The average diameters of OPD-CDs, FOPD-CDs, ClOPD-CDs, BrOPD-CDs, and IOPD-CDs were approximately 2.22, 1.70, 3.68, 2.36, and 2.25 nm, respectively (Fig. 1g-k).

To investigate the surface functional groups and chemical bonds, we examined the FTIR and XPS spectra of five different types of CDs. The FTIR spectrum ( Fig. 2b) showed that all CD samples possessed similar functional groups, such as methylene (2876 and 2973 cm−1), C-N = (1350 cm−1), aromatic C−NH (1261 cm−1), and C-N/C−O (1124 cm−1). These functional groups on their surfaces suggest a common derivation process. Moreover, the characteristic vibration spectra of OPD-CDs provide essential references for understanding the CDs’ structure and their fluorophore. The FTIR spectra of CA and OPD (Fig. 2a) demonstrated the presence of hydroxyl or amino groups (1240, 1633, and 3374 cm−1). Following amidation and carbonization, new stretching vibrations from these hydrophilic groups confirm the formation of new organic functional groups on OPD-CDs [20]. Notably, in the case of OPD-CDs, compared to OPD, there was a prominent decrease in −NH2 stretching vibration between 3200–3300 cm−1 and an enhancement in C-N stretching vibration around 1124 cm−1. This suggests the conversion of most −NH2 groups in OPD into C−N−C structures to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (2,3-DAPN) during thermal processes [7]. However, the FTIR spectra of OPD-CDs also revealed a persistent bending vibration of −NH2, indicating residual −NH2 groups post-thermal processing. Comparing the FTIR spectra of the five samples, functional groups such as C-N=, aromatic C−NH, and C-N/C-O present in the CDs indicated that OPD-type ligands and CA formed CDs with polyaromatic structures via decarboxylation/amidation, polymerization, and carbonization [21].

Surface atomic concentrations of CDs were assessed through XPS element analysis ( Fig. 3a-e). Besides the consistent presence of C, N, and O, halogens were also detected in FOPD-CDs, ClOPD-CDs, BrOPD-CDs, and IOPD-CDs, implying halogen atoms in OPD-type ligands are incorporated into the CDs. XPS results, reinforced by FTIR data, showed C–halogen bond stretching vibrations centered at 540–760 cm–1 (Fig. 2). The high-resolution C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three binding energy peaks at approximately 284.3, 285.8, and 288 eV, corresponding to the C═C/C–C, C–O/C–N, and C═O bonds, respectively (Fig. 3f-j). Carbon atomic ratios varied among IOPD-CDs, FOPD-CDs, ClOPD-CDs, and BrOPD-CDs, indicating distinct carbonization levels. Notably, IOPD-CDs exhibited a lower degree of carbonization but a higher oxygen content compared to ClOPD-CDs, indicating partial carbonization and over-oxidation [22]. In Fig. 3k-o, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum reveals three peaks at 398.60 eV, 400.00 eV, and 401.30 eV, attributed to pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen, respectively, which contribute to π-conjugated systems within graphene-like layers [23–25]. The atomic F:N ratio in FOPD-CDs, approaching 1:2, suggests residual OPD-type ligand presence on CDs’ surface [26]. Results from chemical structure characterizations indicate CDs are dominated by residual −NH2 groups of OPD-type ligands, along with low levels of -OH, CO, -CH2-, and C−N−C structures. Thus, during preparation, OPD-type ligands underwent oxidative polymerization with CA, facilitating formation distribution on CDs.

The emission characteristics of the five CDs, spanning from blue to red, were analyzed through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra [22]. The emission wavelength of PL materials typically relates to the energy gap between their excited and ground states, with these energy levels defined by the materials’ inherent electronic structures [27]. As depicted in Fig. 4a-e, the UV–vis absorption spectra exhibited consistent absorption at 200 nm and 280 nm, which corresponds to π–π* transitions of CC and CN bonds in aromatic sp² domains [28]. Conversely, the absorption spectra for IOPD-CDs, FOPD-CDs, BrOPD-CDs, and ClOPD-CDs displayed distinct broad absorption bands in the ultraviolet range, spanning 300–480 nm, 350–500 nm, 360–520 nm, and 400–550 nm, respectively, which indicate differences in their surface state transitions. In the lower energy region, these CDs showed strong excitation absorption bands at 380, 425, 430, and 450 nm, respectively, attributed to n–π* transitions involving the conjugated structures of the phenazine ring and −NH₂ groups [29,30]. Importantly, the UV–vis absorption band near 230 nm varied with different halogen-doped CDs, indicating unique fluorophores for each type. For chlorine-doped CDs, the red-shift of this band suggests an extended π-electron system within the carbon core. The strong coupling between the π-electron system and surface electronic states modifies the CDs’ overall electronic structure [27]. Based on UV–vis absorption and XPS data, we propose that the extent of changes in the π-electron system due to halogen doping explains the red-shifted PL emission in CDs. An intensified π-electron system narrows the energy gap of π–π* transitions, resulting in a smaller energy gap for the surface states (Fig. 4f) [31], consequently lowering energy levels and causing red-shifted PL emission.

Considerable debate exists in the literature regarding CDs’ structure and their fluorescence emission origins. In this study, the carboxylic acid groups of CA undergo condensation reactions with the amino groups of OPD-type ligands, resulting in the formation of polyamides. At temperatures exceeding 200℃, these polymers experience crosslinking, dehydration, and irreversible carbonization, forming sp² and sp³ hybridized carbon cores that incorporate pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen [32,33]. Additionally, CA is proposed to facilitate ring closure reactions with various α,β-diamines, such as OPD, to produce pyridone derivatives [34,35]. The formation of a halogen-substituted 2-pyridone species and its reaction mechanism was proposed (Fig. S2) [32]. One of these fluorescent molecules, 7-chloro-1-oxo-1,5-dihydrobenzo[4,5]imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-3-carboxylic acid (ClDPCA), was synthesized from CA and ClOPD via hydrothermal synthesis. This molecule should exhibit optical properties similar to CDs. Unfortunately, density functional theory (DFT) calculations of ClDPCA showed emissions at 448 nm ( Fig. 5a; Fig. S3), inconsistent with ClOPD-CDs’ PL peak, indicating ClDPCA isn’t the precise reaction intermediate.

Numerous studies report that two OPD molecules can self-condense to form the dimer 2,3-DAPN (substance 2 in Fig. S4) under acidic conditions [7,36,37]. Dhara et al.[38] synthesized fluorophore-containing structures akin to 2,3-DAPN using various OPD-type structures. For instance, 2,3-diamino-7-chlorophenazine emerged through ClOPD self-condensation. The precursors in their study [38] match those used here, thus 2,3-diamino-7-chlorophenazine may form. These molecules play dominant roles in CDs’ PL performance. To validate this, CDs were synthesized using only pure OPD-type ligands (without CA). Unlike CDs from CA and OPD mixtures, fresh CDs (control group in Fig. S5) didn’t display noticeable multicolor luminescence under UV irradiation, confirming CA’s essential role in long-wavelength fluorescent molecule generation. CA induces ring closure upon reaction with dimers like 2,3-diamino-7-chlorophenazine, resulting in pyridone derivatives (Fig. S4) [34,35]. According to the DFT-based PL spectra (Fig. S6), the reaction between CA and 2,3-DAPN results in a PL emission at 556 nm, corresponding to the optical properties of OPD-CDs. Importantly, the emissions from chlorinated products, ClOPD and 2,3-diamino-7-chlorophenazine, show a redshift to 572 nm. This shift is attributed to band gap narrowing, corroborating the hypothesis that halogen doping induces a red-shift in CDs (Fig. 5b-c). The emission at 572 nm overlaps with that of ClOPD-CDs. Additionally, variations in particle size can affect the HOMO-LUMO gap, with larger particles potentially leading to smaller band gaps and emissions at longer wavelengths. Considering the quantum size effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between carbon-core states and fluorophores, calculated results may not precisely match experiments, but the halogen doping redshift trend is certain. Unlike traditional single-color CDs, our halogen-doped CDs, using OPD ligands, yield a range of distinct color variations from blue to red, attributed to diverse pyridone derivative structures.

These fluorophore intermediates are unstable and degrade rapidly, hindering solid sample acquisition and full CD characterization. Indeed, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs present a broad peak around 25°, indicative of amorphous carbon, and sharp peaks characteristic of ordered, polymeric structures (Fig. S7). Thus, CDs not only possess a certain degree of carbon core structure but also have numerous surface polymeric structures [37]. The proposed CD formation process (Fig. 5) involves OPD-type ligands and CA reacting to produce intermediates (pyridone derivatives). These intermediates further react with OPD-type ligands to generate fluorescence molecules. Over time, these molecules grow and carbonize, forming single-layer graphene-embedded or connected fluorescence molecules to CDs with sp3 bonding. Finally, single-layer graphene stacks into zero-dimensional CDs.

The unique optical properties of CDs have led to their investigation as promising tools in BA sensing, particularly for food spoilage indicators like putrescine. Putrescine, a significant BA formed during protein degradation, is closely linked to microbial activity in food [39]. Its accumulation is indicative of increasing spoilage, making it an effective marker for assessing food freshness [2,39]. As shown in Fig. 6a, upon the addition of putrescine, the fluorescence colors of the five types of CDs changed to varying degrees. Notably, ClOPD-CDs exhibited a significant fluorescence shift from orange-red to yellow under 302 nm UV light, with a ΔE value of 44.66, indicating the most considerable color change (Fig. S8). This fluorescence alteration suggests that ClOPD-CDs can serve as ratiometric probes for the visual detection of putrescine. When excited at a wavelength of 470 nm (as optimized in Fig. S9), ClOPD-CDs initially displayed orange-red fluorescence with an intensity ratio (I554/I608) of 1:2.1. Upon putrescine addition, the yellow fluorescence at 554 nm increased continuously, while the red fluorescence at 608 nm showed only a slight increase, as depicted in Fig. 6b. The significant variation in the intensity ratio between the two emission bands caused a shift in fluorescence color from orange-red to yellow (inset of Fig. 6b), indicating that putrescine can be visually detected by the naked eye under a UV lamp. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I554/I608) exhibited a linear relationship with putrescine concentration, ranging from 1.00 to 30.00 mg/L. Using the linear fitting equation, putrescine was quantified, with a detection limit estimated at 0.48 mg/L (S/N = 3), outperforming most existing sensors for putrescine. Importantly, ClOPD-CDs also showed similar fluorescence responses to other common BAs (Fig. 6c), enabling the monitoring of food spoilage through total BAs content. Compared to other high-performing ratiometric probes such as UiO-66-OH@Tb [40], CDs/CdTe QDs [41], Eu/Tb(HFA)@clay [42], TPCH-mOBPy COF [43], and EuMOF-FITC [1], ClOPD-CDs offer an equivalent or superior detection sensitivity and a faster response to BAs. Moreover, their lower cost and simpler preparation underscore their potential for visual monitoring of effective BAs concentrations to assess food spoilage.

To gain insight into the sensing mechanism of the fluorescent signal changes and the performance of CDs toward putrescine, a series of investigations were conducted. Owing to the basicity of putrescine, the zeta potentials of all CDs shifted from positive to negative charges ( Fig. 7a). The PL spectra of ClOPD-CDs exhibited multiple peaks, possibly due to uncharged fluorophores in protonated states or pyridone derivatives [44]. This structural characterization suggests that ClOPD-CDs contain the C−N − C structure, with emission and absorption peaks at 608 and 460 nm, respectively [45]. These findings coincide with those in Fig. 4c, showing that the red emission of ClOPD-CDs is derived from pyridone fragments in the carbon nuclei. Putrescine addition deprotonates the −NH3 + on the surface of ClOPD-CDs to −NH2, with XPS analysis revealing a surface rich in pyridine-N structures. As shown in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence lifetime of ClOPD-CDs decreases with putrescine addition (an increase in pH), indicating that deprotonation impacts the surface electronic states of the synthesized CDs, aligning with the zeta potential findings. The pH-dependent behavior of ClOPD-CDs was investigated using 3D FL spectra, confirming edge protonation impacts on PL properties. It was observed that ClOPD-CDs predominantly exhibited yellow FL emission (550 nm) under alkaline conditions, while red PL emission was exclusively induced in an acidic environment (Fig. 7c-d; Fig. S10). DFT calculations by Chiang et al. [46] demonstrate that amino-protonation in pyridine-N-doped CDs significantly reduces the photonic transition bandgap by inserting excited-state orbital levels into the n–π* transition gap, causing a red shift in fluorescence emissions.

Additionally, ClOPD-CDs’ HOMO energy levels before and after protonation were assessed with UPS to clarify detailed internal energy level structures. The UPS spectra in full scale are presented in Fig. 8a and d, while the regions near the Fermi energy and secondary electron cut-off for ClOPD-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 8b, c, e, and f. The calculation of ClOPD-CDs’ HOMO energy levels from UPS data follows previous studies [7,19]. With an incident photon energy of 21.2 eV, the pre- and post-protonation HOMO energy levels were 7.68 and 7.94 eV, respectively. Fig. 8g shows absorption edges at 538 nm and 552 nm for ClOPD-CDs pre- and post-protonation. The energy gaps between HOMO and LOMO for unprotonated and protonated ClOPD-CDs, calculated from the relationship E g = 1240/λg, are 2.30 eV and 2.24 eV, respectively. Fig. 7c indicates a shift in FL emission peak from 554 nm to 608 nm due to protonation, corresponding to FL emission bandgaps of 2.26 eV and 2.04 eV, using the equation Eg = hν. Fig. 8h and i depict the internal energy structure and electronic transition changes due to protonation, with HOMO levels rising from 7.68 eV to 7.94 eV and energy gaps narrowing from 2.53 eV to 2.38 eV, thus reducing the FL emission gap from 2.26 eV to 2.04 eV.

Therefore, the findings from the analysis of fluorophore structure, the evolution of states induced by protonation, and the identification of PL origin suggest that protonation alters the PL state of ClOPD-CDs. This process increases their internal HOMO energy levels and decreases the energy gap between HOMO and LOMO levels, thereby triggering red-shifted FL emission. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I554/I608) of ClOPD-CDs remains stable at room temperature, with only a slight decrease observed after 30 days (Fig. S11). The cytotoxicity of ClOPD-CDs was also assessed using a CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. S12, after a 24-hour incubation with a 20 μg/mL concentration of ClOPD-CDs, the HeLa cells exhibited an impressive 90 % viability. This evidence strengthens the claim of low toxicity and demonstrates both the stability and biocompatibility of ClOPD-CDs. These characteristics support their practical applications in food safety assessments and extend their broader potential to environmental sensing, particularly for detecting pollutants and hazardous substances that alter pH or interact with amino groups.

Food spoilage, particularly in meats, leads to the release of volatile BAs and offers a reliable approach to assess the sensing performance of ClOPD-CDs for amine detection [43]. Spoilage of pork, beef, and mutton was monitored in real-time using a homemade fluorescent biosensor as a packaged indicator for in-situ BA evaluation. Meat samples (100 g each) were placed in polypropylene trays, with the fluorescent biosensor affixed to the inside surface of the lids to prevent leakage and direct contact with the sample ( Fig. 9a). Stored at 25℃, fluorescence images of the biosensors were captured at intervals under 302 nm UV light. As illustrated in Fig. 9b, the biosensors changed from orange after 12 h to bright yellow after 24 h for pork and mutton, and after 36 h for beef. As a control, biosensors stored with wet sponges for 54 h at 25℃ exhibited no fluorochromic behavior (Fig. S13).

To associate fluorescence color with meat freshness, digital color signals were compared with TVB-N values, a widely recognized index for meat spoilage [47]. TVB-N levels below 15 mg/100 g indicate freshness, while levels above 25 mg/100 g denote spoilage [43,48]. Over 54 h, TVB-N levels in pork, beef, and mutton increased from 8.84, 8.02, and 10.93 mg/100 g to 31.65, 27.97, and 60.43 mg/100 g, respectively (Fig. 9c-e). For intelligent evaluation and supervision of volatile amines, RGB values of fluorescent biosensors were collected during meat spoilage (Fig. 9a). The G/R values plotted against the TVB-N content showed a strong linear correlation (R² = 0.9386), indicating that RGB outputs can predict TVB-N levels. Based on the linear equation between the G/R ratio and TVB-N content, meat freshness can be easily assessed using our developed Color Analyzer app (Fig. S14). Thus, these fluorescent biosensors could be integrated into innovative food packaging, offering consumers and suppliers a portable, contactless method to assess meat freshness in situ using a smartphone.

In summary, this study successfully developed a halogen-controlled strategy to synthesize multicolor fluorescent CDs for food freshness detection, utilizing simplified one-pot reactions with OPD ligands. The CDs exhibit multicolor fluorescence under UV excitation due to halogen doping. With chlorine being incorporated, we have crafted ClOPD-CDs, a high-performing ratiometric fluorescent probe that effectively improves the sensitivity of visual inspection for putrescine with reduced background interference. Experimental investigations demonstrate that the protonation of edge amino groups in ClOPDs and their interaction with carbon structure decisively undergo a color shift from orange-red to yellow in the presence of BA molecules. Further validation came from an examination of the correlation between TVB-N and RGB outputs of a homemade biosensor, affirming the utility of the ClOPD-CDs as an online fluorochromic probe. With the help of a portable detection device and a smartphone, the sensor has been successfully used for real-time, visual, and quantitative monitoring of food quality. These achievements hold significant potential for enhancing food supply chain management, reducing food wastage, and generating substantial economic value.

Song Guangchun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Chen Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xu Le: Writing – review & editing. Zhang Dequan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zheng Xiaochun: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Gu Minghui: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zheng Zixin: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Li Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key R&D Program of Shandong Province, China (2023CXGC010708), and National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFD2100500).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nantod.2025.102675.
